advantages: Simple, fast, and widely available—scales well to large datasets with efficient implementations and mini-batch variants.
Produces interpretable centroids that summarize each cluster as an average feature profile stakeholders can review.
Hard partitions are easy to operationalize: assign new points to the nearest centroid for routing or personalization.
Works well on roughly spherical, similar-sized clusters in scaled numeric feature space with minimal tuning beyond k and initialization.
disadvantages: Requires choosing k in advance; wrong k yields arbitrary or unusable partitions regardless of low WCSS.
Assumes roughly spherical, equal-variance clusters—fails on elongated, nested, or varying-density structures.
Sensitive to initialization and local minima—different random seeds can produce different solutions.
Uses squared Euclidean geometry—dominated by large-scale features unless scaled; categorical and irregular shapes need other methods.
